satire_attempt: In which the staff engineer, having read three blog posts about hexagonal architecture, achieves enlightenment and announces in standup that he has 'internalized the principles' and therefore no longer needs to consult the cookbook, the linter, or, increasingly, his teammates. His pull requests, however, continue to fail CI. Per the Shipping Gita, this is a lateral move from one form of incompetence (rule-hostage) to another (premature-mastery), which apparently is the verse's whole point — a verse that reads less like spiritual teaching and more like a performance review with a Sanskrit footnote.
verdict_reason: My satire lands a glancing blow on the framing tone but cannot dismiss the underlying claim — to mock the verse I had to RE-STATE it correctly (premature-mastery is the failure mode the verse explicitly warns against) and then complain that it sounded like a performance review. That is mocking the genre, not the claim. The verse names a real, falsifiable distinction (artifact-fluency vs. self-reported-fluency) which is not undermined by my satire. Per calibration: 'satire is funny but only because it is unfair to the verse' ⇒ PASS.
